results.possibleIdentity: Larry Boyce (1946–1992) was a prominent American decorative painter and stencil artist known for his significant role in the late-20th-century Victorian revival. He became active in the artistic community in the late 1960s and early 1970s, particularly in the Pacific Northwest and San Francisco Bay Area. Boyce was famously an itinerant artist who traveled more than 200,000 miles by bicycle across the United States to paint elaborate stenciled friezes, ceilings, and moldings in historic homes. His lifestyle and artistry earned him national attention, including coverage in Smithsonian Magazine and the New York Times. His archival papers and documentation are preserved in the Jack Eyerly collection on Pacific Northwest Art at Willamette University. Notably, Jack Eyerly was the founder and secretary of the Portland chapter of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), and Boyce is categorized as an individual artist within this collection, which covers his work and correspondence from approximately 1971 to 1975.
